text: Three dimensional (3D) cage structures comprising $sp^3$-hybridized carbon atoms, specifically [1.1.1]-bicyclopentanes (BCPs), [2.1.1]-bicyclohexanes (BCHs), [3.1.1]-bicycloheptanes (BCHeps) and cubanes, have become valuable bioisosteres for planar heteroaromatic rings in drug development, due to their improved solubility and reduced metabolism. In this review, we summarize recent accomplishments in light-driven synthesis and modification of these three-dimensional cage molecules. A variety of photochemical methods such as visible light photocatalysts and energy transfer (EnT) pathways, direct photoexcitation as well as electron donor-acceptor (EDA) complex activation are discussed. Key features include the development of photochemical methods that yield the propellanes can be opened by radicals to afford BCPSs, the site-selective functionalization of the bridgehead and bridge sites, as well as the design of highly stereoselective and DNA-compatible photochemical reactions. In addition, the challenges and potential applications of 3D cage molecules in the field of medicinal chemistry and materials science will be discussed, in order to summarize the future development of the cage molecules.
